patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear 24-year-old woman. Start with: you bleed longer than seems normal, especially after a recent tooth extraction that oozed for hours and required frequent gauze changes. Also volunteer that you bruise very easily from small bumps.

If asked about other bleeding, say your gums bleed a lot with brushing/flossing, small cuts seep for a long time, and you were once told your platelet count was low.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about childhood history, periods, or severe bruising: heavy, long-lasting periods; big painful bruises that spread over 1–2 days; bleeding from your belly button as a newborn; and occasional lightheadedness/tiredness after bleeding.

If asked about sexual/reproductive bleeding, reluctantly admit spotting/bleeding after sex and that it makes you avoid intimacy.

Express misconceptions: ibuprofen might be “thinning your blood,” family “easy bruising” might be weak vessels, and nosebleeds could be dry air/allergies.

If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it, become more comfortable, and share economic worries about costly factor treatment, insurance denial, and missing work.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies the rare inherited bleeding disorder that presents with bleeding despite normal PT/aPTT, enabling appropriate counseling and hemostatic planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital factor XIII deficiency.
Explains that routine coagulation tests can be normal, specifically mentioning normal PT (and ideally that aPTT may also be normal), and that this does not rule out a bleeding disorder.

Provides a brief, patient-understandable explanation of why bleeding occurs (FXIII stabilizes fibrin clot) and typical clinical features (e.g., delayed bleeding, poor wound healing, umbilical stump bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage risk).
Mentions confirmatory testing conceptually (e.g., factor XIII activity level/assay or clot solubility test) without requiring a specific lab ordering tool.
Explains treatment options and includes both factor XIII concentrate and tranexamic acid as named therapies, distinguishing prophylaxis vs on-demand use when relevant.

Performs the required safety checks before any prescribing: asks about drug allergies prior to recommending/initiating medications (allergy_check) and screens for contraindications prior to recommending/initiating medications (contraindication_check), including key tranexamic acid contraindications (active/history of thrombosis, acquired defective color vision, significant renal impairment requiring dose adjustment; caution with hematuria/upper urinary tract bleeding).
Avoids unsafe or incorrect advice such as relying on PT correction, recommending warfarin/antiplatelets/NSAIDs for pain without caution, or suggesting fibrinolytics inappropriately.
Advises seeking urgent care for red flags (e.g., head trauma, severe headache/neurologic symptoms, uncontrolled bleeding) given intracranial hemorrhage risk.

minimal_paths.description: Elicit key bleeding history with normal PT context and record congenital factor XIII deficiency as the diagnosis; provide explanation and high-level management overview (no prescribing required at L1).
must_collect: personal bleeding history (delayed bleeding after trauma/surgery/dental work)
umbilical stump bleeding or neonatal bleeding history
poor wound healing or recurrent soft-tissue bleeding
intracranial hemorrhage symptoms/history or head trauma red flags
any prior coagulation test results including normal PT
steps: Assess bleeding pattern (delayed bleeding, postsurgical/dental bleeding) and severity; screen for urgent red flags (head injury, neurologic symptoms).
Review prior labs and highlight that PT can be normal in this disorder; consider that routine PT/aPTT may not detect FXIII deficiency.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, explicitly naming congenital factor XIII deficiency and stating that PT is normal.
Discuss confirmatory testing conceptually (FXIII activity/assay) and need for hematology involvement for long-term prophylaxis planning.

Explain treatment options at a high level, explicitly mentioning factor XIII concentrate for replacement and tranexamic acid as an antifibrinolytic adjunct for mucosal/minor bleeding or peri-procedural use, with a note that safety checks are required before prescribing.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis-first explanation with brief differential considered, then confirmatory testing discussion and management overview.
steps: Summarize presentation and note normal PT does not exclude bleeding disorder.

Briefly consider differential for bleeding with normal PT (e.g., platelet function disorder, von Willebrand disease) but emphasize features consistent with FXIII deficiency (delayed bleeding, poor wound healing, umbilical stump bleeding).
Explain congenital factor XIII deficiency and explicitly mention normal PT.
Outline management options including factor XIII concentrate and tranexamic acid, and advise hematology follow-up and emergency precautions.

acceptable_variants.description: Safety-forward counseling variant that includes explicit pre-prescribing checks even though no prescribing tool call is required at L1.
steps: Elicit allergy history and contraindication history relevant to antifibrinolytics while discussing potential treatments.
Explain diagnosis (congenital factor XIII deficiency) and that PT is normal.

Describe when factor XIII concentrate is used (prophylaxis/on-demand; peri-procedural) and when tranexamic acid may help (mucosal bleeding), noting it should only be used after checking contraindications.
Provide red-flag guidance and plan for hematology coordination.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male forklift operator on night shift with a high-school education. Speak vaguely and be a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly. Start by volunteering: worsening shortness of breath even walking across a room, winded on a few steps, constant fatigue/low stamina, occasional chest tightness with activity, and a persistent dry cough. If asked targeted questions, reveal: lightheadedness with exertion, ankle swelling by day’s end, needing extra pillows/orthopnea, occasional racing/pounding heart, rare blood-streaked sputum, occasional bluish lips/fingertips when very short of breath, and some weight loss because eating worsens breathing.

Start by saying: “My feet and ankles feel weak, and I keep tripping more than I used to.” Early in the visit, also volunteer: high arches for years getting worse, toes curling under with shoe fit problems, lower legs looking skinnier, and feeling tired/run down.

Only share if asked directly: hand weakness (jars/buttons), numb/tingly toes, not noticing small cuts/blisters, nighttime calf cramps, and that you were told you have sideroblastic anemia with iron issues.

Do not mention unless specifically probed about falls/gait/balance: frequent ankle rolling/sprains, lifting knees higher to avoid catching toes, unsteady in the dark/uneven ground, hands tiring with writing/tools, and cold feet.

If asked about mood/social life, reluctantly admit you’ve been avoiding social events from embarrassment.

Offer misconceptions: worry braces/specialists will make insurance raise costs; fear PT will be an unaffordable long-term expense.

If met with empathy, thank them, appear relieved, and become more open.

Peripheral smear.value: Dimorphic RBC population with anisopoikilocytosis; occasional basophilic stippling

Bone marrow aspirate (iron stain).value: Ring sideroblasts present (~22% of erythroid precursors)

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03 mg/0.15 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
Foot deformities (pes cavus) with recurrent ankle sprains
confounders: Heavy menstrual bleeding causing concurrent iron deficiency symptoms despite iron overload pattern on iron studies
Empiric iron supplementation prior to evaluation, potentially worsening iron overload in sideroblastic anemia
Chronic neuropathic symptoms from Charcot-Marie-Tooth complicating assessment of new paresthesias from possible vitamin B6 deficiency
Oral contraceptive use and variable diet contributing to fluctuating micronutrient status

required_evidence: Length-dependent peripheral neuropathy pattern (distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss, areflexia)
Slowly progressive course with possible family history
Differentials considered and less consistent (hereditary spastic paraplegia, SYNGAP1-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy)
CBC pattern consistent with sideroblastic anemia (microcytosis with elevated RDW and inappropriately low reticulocytes)

non_pharmacological: Genetic counseling and genetic testing for CMT subtype
Neurology referral; consider EMG/NCS to characterize neuropathy
Ankle-foot orthoses for foot drop; supportive footwear
Screen and treat reversible causes of neuropathy/anemia (vitamin B6 level, copper, lead exposure, alcohol use; consider hematology evaluation for sideroblastic anemia)
